coredata.dc:title: Synergistic Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl composite electrolyte for high-performance all-solid-state lithium batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co., Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuzhe
Chang, Haolong
Han, Aiguo
Xu, Shijie
Wang, Xinyu
Yang, Shunjin
Hu, Xiaohu
Sun, Yujiang
Sun, Xiao
Chen, Xing
Yang, Yongan
coredata.dc:description: Li6PS5Cl is a highly wanted sulfide-solid-electrolyte (SSE) for developing all-solid-state lithium batteries, due to its high ionic conductivity, good processability and abundant compositional elements. However, its cyclability is poor because of harmful side reactions at the Li6PS5Cl/Li interface and growth of lithium dendrites inside Li6PS5Cl phase. Herein, we report a simple interface-engineering remedy to boost the electrochemical performance of Li6PS5Cl, by coating its surface with a Li-compatible electrolyte Li3OCl having low electronic conductivity. The obtained Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure exhibits a synergistic effect. Consequently, compared with the bare Li6PS5Cl, this composite electrolyte exhibits great performance improvements: 1) In Li|electrolyte|Li symmetric cells, the critical current density at 30 °C gets increased from 0.6 mA cm−2 to 1.6 mA cm−2, and the lifetime gets prolonged from 320 h to 1400 h at the cycling current of 0.2 mA cm−2 or from 10 h to 900 h at the cycling current of 0.5 mA cm−2; 2) In Li|electrolyte|NCM721 full cells running at 30 °C, the cycling capacity at 0.2 C (or 0.5 C) gets enhanced by 20% (or from unfeasible to be feasible) for 100 cycles and the rate capability reaches up to 2 C from 0.2 C; and in full cells running at 60 °C, the cycling capacity is increased by 7% at 0.2 C and the rate capability is enhanced to 3.0 C from 0.5 C. The experimental studies and theoretical computations show that the performance enhancements are due to the confined electron penetration and suppressed lithium dendrites growth at the Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl interface.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li6PS5Cl
Li3OCl
Composite solid electrolytes
All-solid-state lithium batteries
Synergism
Core@Shell structure

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFE0111600). We thank the Haihe Laboratory of Sustainable Chemical Transformations for financial support. This work was also partially supported by the Graduate Top-notch Innovation Award Plan in Liberal Arts and Science of Tianjin University for the Year of 2023 (B2-2023-012). This work is dedicated to the 130th anniversary of Tianjin University and the 100th anniversary of its Discipline of Chemical Engineering and Technology.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFE0111600). We thank the Haihe Laboratory of Sustainable Chemical Transformations for financial support (YYJC202104). This work was also partially supported by the Graduate Top-notch Innovation Award Plan in Liberal Arts and Science of Tianjin University for the Year of 2023 (B2-2023-012).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the process for making the Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl composite electrolyte. (a) The step of synthesizing Li3OCl; (b) The step of compositing Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl.
(a) XRD patterns of Li3OCl, Li6PS5Cl and their composite electrolytes. (b) EIS plot of Li3OCl, (c) EIS plots of Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl composite electrolytes.

(a) Schematic illustration of the CCD measurement setup using lithium symmetric cells with a pressure monitor. (b) The voltage-time profiles for the CCD studies of Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (3, 5 and 7 wt%)|Li symmetric cells at 30 °C and CCD of Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li and Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li symmetric cells at 30 °C. (c) The voltage-time profiles for repeatedly plating/stripping lithium in Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li and Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li symmetric cells at 0.2 mA cm−2 and 30 °C. (d) The voltage-time profiles for repeatedly plating/stripping lithium in Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li and Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li symmetric cells at 0.5 mA cm−2 and 30 °C.
(a) Schematic diagram of Li|electrolyte|NCM721 full cells. (b) The charge/discharge profiles at 30 °C and 0.2 C. (c) Cycling stability at 30 °C and 0.2 C. (d) Rate capability at 30 °C.

SEM images of (a) Li6PS5Cl and (b) Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl; TEM images of (c) Li6PS5Cl and (d–e) Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl at lower and higher magnifications respectively; (f–l) EDS mappings of elements P, S, O and Cl for Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl.

XPS spectrum of S 2p for (a) Li6PS5Cl and (b) Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl; CV curves of (c) Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li6PS5Cl + C and (d) Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl + C semi-blocking cells; EIS plots of (e) Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li and (f) Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li after aging for different durations.

Comparison of electronic structure between Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl and Li6PS5Cl. (a) Charge density differences of the interface model Li6PS5Cl/Li. (b) Charge difference of Li6PS5Cl/Li along the z-direction. (c) Charge density differences of the interface model Li6PS5Cl/Li3OCl. (d) Charge difference of Li6PS5Cl/Li3OCl along the z-direction.

head.ce:title: Synergistic Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl composite electrolyte for high-performance all-solid-state lithium batteries

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Li6PS5Cl is a highly wanted sulfide-solid-electrolyte (SSE) for developing all-solid-state lithium batteries, due to its high ionic conductivity, good processability and abundant compositional elements. However, its cyclability is poor because of harmful side reactions at the Li6PS5Cl/Li interface and growth of lithium dendrites inside Li6PS5Cl phase. Herein, we report a simple interface-engineering remedy to boost the electrochemical performance of Li6PS5Cl, by coating its surface with a Li-compatible electrolyte Li3OCl having low electronic conductivity. The obtained Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure exhibits a synergistic effect. Consequently, compared with the bare Li6PS5Cl, this composite electrolyte exhibits great performance improvements: 1) In Li|electrolyte|Li symmetric cells, the critical current density at 30 °C gets increased from 0.6 mA cm−2 to 1.6 mA cm−2, and the lifetime gets prolonged from 320 h to 1400 h at the cycling current of 0.2 mA cm−2 or from 10 h to 900 h at the cycling current of 0.5 mA cm−2; 2) In Li|electrolyte|NCM721 full cells running at 30 °C, the cycling capacity at 0.2 C (or 0.5 C) gets enhanced by 20% (or from unfeasible to be feasible) for 100 cycles and the rate capability reaches up to 2 C from 0.2 C; and in full cells running at 60 °C, the cycling capacity is increased by 7% at 0.2 C and the rate capability is enhanced to 3.0 C from 0.5 C. The experimental studies and theoretical computations show that the performance enhancements are due to the confined electron penetration and suppressed lithium dendrites growth at the Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl interface.

Herein, a simple interface-engineering remedy to boost the electrochemical performance of Li6PS5Cl is reported, by coating its surface with a Li-compatible electrolyte Li3OCl having low electronic conductivity. Due to a synergistic effect, the obtained Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure exhibits great performance improvements compared with the bare Li6PS5Cl.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium ion batteries (LIBs) based on liquid electrolytes have been widely used in fields of portable electronic devices, electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems, etc., bringing great convenience to various aspects of human life [1,2]. However, the existing LIBs have approached the theoretical limits of their energy density, and the risk of thermal runaway has not been fully controlled. All-solid-state lithium batteries (ASSLBs) using solid-electrolytes (SEs) among the next generation of batteries have emerged as one of the most promising candidates for addressing the above issues [3–5]. Among all SEs, a sulfide-solid-electrolyte (SSE) Li6PS5Cl electrolyte stands out, because of its high ionic conductivity (∼10 mS cm−1), good processability (due to its moderate hardness) and abundant compositional elements (of which P, S and Cl are ranked at the 11th, 17th and 21st, respectively, among all earth-crust elements), attracting great attention from researchers [6,7]. However, at the interface between lithium metal and electrolyte, the formation of lithium dendrites and the interfacial reaction between lithium metal and Li6PS5Cl electrolyte result in the deterioration of battery performances [8,9]. The spontaneously reactions between PS4 3− tetrahedrons in Li6PS5Cl and lithium metal produce Li2S and Li3P. Consequently, Li6PS5Cl cannot inhibit lithium dendrites and then presents a small critical current density (CCD), which means that, Li6PS5Cl would be short-circuited by lithium dendrites and die even under a low current density.

To improve the electrochemical stability and interface stability of Li6PS5Cl electrolyte, researchers have developed many strategies, which can be mainly divided into two categories such as the construction of surface engineering and bulk element doping, etc. [10–12] The former strategy can increase its stability to lithium metal and reduce the interface side reactions. Representatively, Zhou et al. synthesized Li6PS5Cl-OF0.25 electrolyte, which had a LiF@Li2O shell structure and exhibited a “one stone killing three birds” function with improved moisture tolerance, interface compatibility with lithium metal and stability against LiCoO2 [13]. The Li6PS5Cl-OF0.25 electrolyte has a high CCD value of 2 mA cm−2 at 60 °C, which is much higher than that of pristine Li6PS5Cl (1 mA cm−2). After 100 cycles, the capacity retention rate of the Li–In|Li6PS5Cl-OF0.25|LCO cell increased to 100% from 26.5% for the Li–In|Li6PS5Cl|LCO cell. More remarkably, Wang et al. reported in Nature that an interlayer of Mg16Bi84 at the Li/Li6PS5Cl interface can greatly inhibit the growth of Li dendrites, and an F-rich interlayer at the NMC811/Li6PS5Cl interface can dramatically reduce the interface resistance therein [14]. Consequently, they achieved high energy (15.3 mA cm−2) and fast charging (6 mAh cm−2) at low stacking pressure of 2.5 MPa. Regarding the element doping strategy, many elements have been studied [15]. For instance, Liu et al. proposed redox-resistible electrolyte by co-doping Li6PS5Cl with Mg and F, which can regulate the electron density distribution in the solid electrolyte and in situ generate LiF-rich interlayer to regulate lithium plating/stripping [16]. The CCD of Li|Li6PS5Cl-MF|Li symmetrical cell reaches 1.4 mA cm−2, representing an improvement of 133% compared to the pristine Li6PS5Cl electrolyte. In addition, Li|Li6PS5Cl-MF|LCO all-solid-state batteries exhibit excellent cycling stability and rate performance. Duan et al. reported a dual-substitution strategy to make Sn- and O-doped Li6PS5Cl, i.e., Li5.5(P0.9Sn0.1)(S4.2O0.2)Cl1.6, for improving the moisture tolerance and boost the electrochemical performance [17]. Li5.5(P0.9Sn0.1)(S4.2O0.2)Cl1.6 exhibited a high ionic conductivity of 8.7 mS cm−1 and an increased CCD value from 1 mA cm−2 to 1.2 mA cm−2 in lithium metal symmetric battery. Li–In|Li5.5(P0.9Sn0.1)(S4.2O0.2)Cl1.6|NMC811 full cells show an high initial discharge capacity of 104 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and maintains 101 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles, while discharge capacity of pristine Li6PS5Cl decreased to 64 mAh g−1.

Among all Li-compatible electrolytes, Li3OCl probably has the simplest compositions. It exists in the anti-perovskite structure and presents many advantages, such as low electronic conductivity, wide electrochemical window (>5 V), good compatibility also with high voltage cathodes, low cost and environmental friendliness [18,19]. These merits are complementary to the drawbacks of Li6PS5Cl [20]. However, Li3OCl suffers from two issues that it instantly hydrolyzes in humid air and that its Li-ion conductivity is low (∼10−2 mS cm−1), making it unable to enter the mainstream of SEs. Thus, it is interesting to study if Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl can complement with the shortcomings of each other to make a synergistic composite electrolyte [21–23].

Herein, this article reports a Li6PS5Cl-based advanced composite electrolyte with the Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure made by a simple procedure of manually grinding their powders together (Fig. 1 ), wherein the Li3OCl-synthesis was adapted from literature [24]. The shell is only 50 nm thick, while the core is about 6 μm big. Amazingly, the CCD value of the Li|electrolyte|Li symmetric cells at 30 °C gets significantly increased, and the lifetime gets greatly prolonged. More importantly, in Li|electrolyte|NCM721 full cells, the cycling capacity and the rate capability at both 30 °C and 60 °C get highly boosted. We believe that the synergistic effect comes from two reasons that the inner core Li6PS5Cl of high ionic conductivity maintains high currents and that the outer shell Li3OCl of low electronic conductivity suppresses the growth of lithium dendrites as well as the interfacial side reactions. The effectiveness of the Li3OCl layer was confirmed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements and theoretical calculations.

Fig. 2 (a) displays XRD patterns of Li3OCl, Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl with different Li3OCl mass fractions. The XRD patterns of Li3OCl in Fig. 2(a) are consistent with the literature data, confirming its successful preparation [25]. Besides, due to the hygroscopicity of Li3OCl, there are several peaks of Li5(OH)2Cl3 impurity at 30.8° and 34.3° [26]. The XRD patterns of commercial Li6PS5Cl are also consistent with the Li6PS5Cl standard (PDF #97-041-8490) [27]. When increasing the mass fraction of Li3OCl to 7% in the composite SEs, the peaks of Li3OCl and Li5(OH)2Cl3 at 32.2° and 34.3° become detectable. Fig. 2(b) shows the EIS spectrum of pure Li3OCl. The intersection of the left end of the semicircle on the Z′-axis corresponds to the grain resistance, and the right end corresponds to the total resistance (grain resistance plus the interface resistance) [28], with the values to be 2.9 kΩ and 22 kΩ, respectively. From these values, the grain conductivity of Li3OCl can be extracted to be 4.4 ×10−2 mS cm−1 and the total conductivity of Li3OCl to be 4.2 ×10−3 mS cm−1 according to Eqn. (1) in supporting information, consistent with the literature report [29]. Regarding the Li6PS5Cl-based composite electrolytes (as shown in Fig. 2(c)), the impedance values are much smaller despite growing with the increase of Li3OCl mass fraction. The calculation shows that the ionic conductivity values of Li6PS5Cl, Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (3 wt%), Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (5 wt%) and Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (7 wt%) are 8.9 mS cm−1, 8.6 mS cm−1, 7.3 mS cm−1 and 4.8 mS cm−1, respectively. We think such decrease of ionic conductivity may not be problematic, because many workable Li6PS5Cl electrolytes in the literature had ionic conductivity below 5 mS cm−1 [30]. The electronic conductivities of Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl are 7.9 × 10−6 mS cm−1 and 6.4 × 10−12 mS cm−1, respectively (Fig. S1). Remarkably, coating the Li3OCl shell outside the Li6PS5Cl core reduces the electronic conductivity of the pristine Li6PS5Cl by one order of magnitude, to 5.1× 10−7 mS cm−1 for Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (3 wt%), 5.3 × 10−7 mS cm−1 for Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (5 wt%) and 3.7 × 10−7 mS cm−1 for Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (7 wt%), respectively.

Then, Li|electrolyte|Li symmetric cells were constructed to evaluate the electrolyte stability against lithium metal, by measuring CCD. The setup is schematically shown in Fig. 3 (a), mainly consisting of a mold cell and a pressure monitor. On the basis of our experience, the holding pressure was controlled at 10 MPa. As the current density gradually increases to CCD, the corresponding overpotential will continuously increase until a short circuit occurs, resulting in a sudden drop in voltage. Fig. 3(b) reveals that the CCD values for three types of composite electrolytes are all higher than that of pristine electrolyte, i.e., 1.0 mA cm−2 (3 wt%), 1.6 mA cm−2 (5 wt%), and 0.8 mA cm−2 (7 wt%) versus 0.6 mA cm−2. These results strongly indicate that the addition of Li3OCl has indeed significantly increased the compatibility of Li6PS5Cl against Li [31,32], with 2.67 times of enhancement for the optimal electrolyte. Note that, for comparison, we also tried preparing composite electrolytes by ball milling the mixture of Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl with 4 wt% and 6 wt% of Li3OCl. Consequently (Fig. S2(a)), the CCD value of pure Li6PS5Cl after ball-milling decreased to as low as 0.2 mA cm−2. Although the Li3OCl coating can enhance CCD, the 4 wt% and 6 wt% loadings of Li3OCl are unable to counteract the negative impact caused by ball-milling, i.e., still lower than the CCD of the pristine Li6PS5Cl. Only Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (5 wt%) shows higher CCD than the pristine Li6PS5Cl (Fig. S2(b)), but still just 0.7 mA cm−2, significantly lower than that of hand-ground Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (5 wt%). The underlying reasons for the above-mentioned experimental phenomena can be attributed to the mechanical damage to the ball-milled materials, for instance, powder fragmentation and crystalline defects. Thus, all the subsequent investigations were carried out on Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl (5%) made by hand-grinding, unless stated otherwise. Fig. 3(c) shows the lifetime studies on Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li and Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li symmetric cells by plating/stripping lithium at 0.2 mA cm−2 and 30 °C. With cycling, the polarization voltage of the Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li symmetric cell increased gradually from 7.1 mV to 38.8 mV and ultimately became short-circuited at 320 h. In stark contrast, the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li symmetric cell can cycle stably for >1400 h at a stable overpotential in the range of 7.1–10 mV, showing more than four times of life extension. When cycling at 0.5 mA cm−2 and 30 °C, the Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li symmetric cell died after only 10 h. Very excitingly, the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li symmetric cell can survive over 900 h at a steady overpotential of 17 mV, as shown in Fig. 3(d). The symmetric cell performance of our Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl composite electrolyte is compared with relevant studies in literature on improving the performance of Li6PS5Cl, as shown in Fig. S3, showing that our Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl electrolyte is at the upstream level among all the works [33–39]. Such amazing performance enhancement was serendipitous and can be assigned to the synergistic effect between the Li6PS5Cl core and the Li3OCl shell, whose structural confirmation will be presented later.

Then, Li|electrolyte|NCM721 full cells were constructed (Fig. 4 (a)) to further evaluate the performance of Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl electrolytes. Fig. 4(b) presents the voltage–capacity profiles in the range of 2.6 V–4.35 V for charging/discharging at 30 °C and 0.2 C. While the Li|Li6PS5Cl|NCM721 full cell exhibits the capacity at 128 mAh g−1, the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|NCM721 full cell gets increased to 163 mAh g−1, we speculate that the above capacity difference can be attributed to the weakened interfacial reactions between lithium metal and electrolytes under protection of Li3OCl [40,41]. Within the subsequent 100 cycles (Fig. 4(c)), while both cells can cycle steadily without obvious degradation and present comparable coulombic efficiencies, the capacity of the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|NCM721 cell is 25% higher than that of the Li|Li6PS5Cl|NCM721 cell, i.e., ∼155 mAh g−1 versus ∼128 mAh g−1, which performs well in comparison with recently reported Li6PS5Cl electrolytes [42–46]. Next, the rate capability tests at 30 °C show that the Li|Li6PS5Cl|NCM721 cell gets short circuited at only 0.5 C; but the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|NCM721 cell can perform well even at 2.0 C ((Fig. 4(d))). For the Li|Li6PS5Cl|NCM721 cell, its capacity retention ratio is 91% at 0.2 C before death at 0.5 C. In contrast, the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|NCM721 cell presents much higher capacity retention ratios, i.e., 92% at 0.2 C (160 mAh g−1), 81% at 0.5 C (141 mAh g−1), 74% at 1 C (128 mAh g−1), 30% at 2.0 C (52 mAh g−1). When the rate returns to 0.1 C, the capacity comes back to 171 mAh g−1 with a recovery rate of 98%. Subsequently, cycling stability tests were conducted at a higher rate of 0.5 C, as shown in Fig. S4(a). The Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|NCM721 cell exhibits an initial capacity of 168 mAh g−1 with the Coulombic efficiency of 76%. After 100 cycles, its capacity remains at 132 mAh g−1, corresponding to a capacity retention ratio of 79%. In contrast, the Li|Li6PS5Cl|NCM721 cell was short circuited and unable to cycle at all. Fig. S4(b) illustrates the drastic difference between the voltage-time curves for the first cycle of both cells, wherein the sudden drop in voltage represents the occurrence of short circuit. Furthermore, the electrochemical performance of Li|electrolyte|NCM721 full cells were also evaluated at a higher temperature of 60 °C (Fig. S5). The specific capacity of Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|NCM721 full cell at the rate of 0.2 C starts with 7% higher than that of Li|Li6PS5Cl|NCM721, i.e. 175 mAh g−1 versus 163 mAh g−1. Subsequently, the former also decays slower than the latter, showing a higher capacity retention ratio of 90.9% versus 81.6% after 100 cycles (Fig. S5(a)). As for the rate capability (Fig. S5(b), the Li|Li6PS5Cl|NCM721 cell starts from 154 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C, decreases to 145 mAh g−1 at 0.2 C, 125 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and dies at 1.0 C. In stark contrast, the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|NCM721 cell shows higher capacity at all rates of 0.1 C (190 mAh g−1), 0.2 C (183 mAh g−1), 0.5 C (170 mAh g−1), 1.0 C (158 mAh g−1), 2.0 C (138 mAh g−1) and 3.0 C (126 mAh g−1). Moreover, the retention ratios are also higher, i.e., 96% versus 91% at 0.2 C, 88% versus 78% at 0.5 C, and 83% versus 0% at 1.0 C. In the case of Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|NCM721, when the rate returns to 0.1 C, the capacity comes back to 185 mAh g−1 with a high recovery rate of 97%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation of the Li6PS5Cl @ Li3OCl Core@Shell structure
ce:section.ce:para: After observing the enhanced performance for the Li3OCl-composited Li6PS5Cl electrolyte, SEM, TEM and EDS were used to characterize the electrolyte structure. The SEM images show that the pristine Li6PS5Cl is composed of coal-grain like particles with irregular-shapes and -sizes (Fig. S6(a)) and that the hand-ground Li6PS5Cl does not cause morphological changes (Fig. S6(b)). On the contrary, the pristine Li3OCl consists of peanuts-shaped (sub)micrometer-sized primary particles which aggregate into plain-shaped secondary particles of tens-of-micrometer in size (Fig. S6(c)), and the manually-ground Li3OCl spreads into even larger laminated blocks losing the clear feature of primary particles (Fig. S6(d)). The TEM image in Fig. S7(a) show that the individual primary particle of the pristine Li3OCl is about 1.5 μm big and spheroidal with coastline-like contour. Differently, the TEM image of the hand-ground Li3OCl (Fig. S7(b)) show a quite-round disc-like particle of about 5.5 μm in size. The above phenomena may somehow explain why the hand feeling on grinding Li3OCl is of less friction, better ductility and lower hardness than grinding Li6PS5Cl. After grinding Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl together (Fig. 5 (b)), the composite material turns into better isolated particles with smoother surfaces. These results imply that hand-grinding has caused Li3OCl to coat on the surface of Li6PS5Cl. In contrast, the ball-milling prepared composite of Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl (denoted as bm_composite) did not form a smooth surface (Fig. S8) and did not present high CCD either (Fig. S2). In addition, we have prepared a comparative sample by mixing Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl powders via simple spoon-stirring (denoted as ss_composite), but not via comprehensive hand-grinding as in the case of making Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl samples (denoted as hg_composite). As shown by SEM images in Fig. S9, ss_composite (a) and hg_composite (b) exhibit quite different particle morphologies. The corresponding EDS mappings for ss_composite (Fig. S10) show that the signal distributions of P and S do not fully overlap with those of O and Cl. While the observed O-signals can be mainly ascribed to surface oxidation caused by air-exposure during the sample preparation, three circle-marked areas do not have signals of P and S, but only signals of O and Cl. Thus, the particles therein must be only Li3OCl, and the other areas are mainly Li6PS5Cl. In stark contrast, the EDS mappings for hg_composite (Fig. S11) illustrate that elements of P, S, O and Cl are all evenly and coherently distributed throughout the entire signal areas. Therefore, the hg_composite sample is not likely to be a simple mixture of separated Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl particles. The speculated Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure is further supported by the TEM analysis, of which Fig. 5(c) shows a sphere-shaped particle with non-uniform density for the pristine Li6PS5Cl and Fig. 5(d) shows a core@shell particle for the Li3OCl-composited Li6PS5Cl. The shell is about 30–50 nm thick. To gain a better understanding, the high-angle annual dark field (HAADF) technique was used to zoom in on a smaller area of the particle surface (Fig. 5(e)). The corresponding EDS mappings on P, S, O and Cl (Fig. 5(f-l)) reveal that P and S elements are uniformly distributed only in the core, but O and Cl elements are uniformly distributed in both the core and the shell, matching well with the morphological profile. For the pristine Li6PS5Cl, EDS mappings of elements P, S and Cl show consistent profiles with its morphology (Fig. S12), different from core@shell features. Thus, at this point, we can conclude that all the structural characterizations together confirm the Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure.

To shed light on the performance-enhancing mechanism, both Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl were studied with XPS, CV, EIS and DFT computations. Before XPS tests, the sample surfaces were bombarded with Ar+ ions for 252 s to etch off ∼45-nm thick top layer in order to reach the Li6PS5Cl/Li3OCl interface. There are no detectable differences in Li, P and Cl elements (Fig. S13), only the XPS spectra of S 2p with obvious difference are shown here (Fig. 6 (a-b)). Both samples exhibit the expected doublet peaks centered at 162.9 eV and 161.5 eV, by referring to literature [47,48]. Besides, in Fig. 6(b), a new set of peaks appear at 161.5 eV and 160.2 eV, close to the values for S in the form of Li2S [50,51]. Thus, such change implies an electron-donating effect of the Li3OCl shell to the surface S atoms of Li6PS5Cl. As shown in Fig. S14, XPS spectra of O 1s for Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl at different depths can also prove the existence of core@shell structure. The O element was tested as the indicator because it is unique to Li3OCl but absent in Li6PS5Cl. For Li6PS5Cl, samples with different etching depths (0 nm, 45 nm, 90 nm) exhibit O 1s peaks at 531.8 eV (Figs. S14a, c, e), which actually come from the conductive adhesive for fixing the XPS sample. For Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl, three O 1s spectra each shows three peaks at 533.3 eV, 531.2 eV and 530.5 eV (Figs. S14b, d, f), which can be attributed to O–Cl bonds, oxygen from conductive adhesive and O–Li bonds, respectively [31]. Evidently, with extending the etching duration, the peak intensities of O–Cl and O–Li decrease versus the oxygen signals from the conductive adhesive, verifying the core@shell structure for Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl.

Next, CV was employed to investigate the redox behaviors of both electrolytes, by constructing Li|electrolyte|electrolyte + C cells. For the negative-scanning CVs in the range of 0.01–0.65 V, neither electrolyte shows obvious peaks, but both present larger current/charge in reduction than oxidation (Fig. 6(c)). The quantitative integration calculation results of the CV curves indicate that the redox charge of Li6PS5Cl-based cell is about twice that of Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl-based cell. These phenomena, consistent with literature [16], indicate an irreversible and gradual reduction of the electrolyte surface by lithium to build up a protective solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. Differently, in the positive-scanning CVs in the range of 0.65–5 V, both electrolytes show not only oxidation peaks but also reduction peaks (Fig. 6(c)). The larger charge quantity for the oxidation process than for the reduction process indicates the formation of a protective cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) layer, according to literature [49]. The two oxidation peaks at ∼2.5 V and ∼3.3 V can be assigned to the delithiation of Li6PS5Cl into S–Li3PS4–LiCl and S–P2S5–LiCl, respectively [50,51]. In the backward scans, two reduction peaks at 1.9 V and 1.6 V correspond to consecutive steps of lithiating Li6PS5Cl into Li2S–Li3P–LiCl. Nevertheless, in both negative-scanning and positive-scanning CVs (Fig. 6(c-d)), the Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl-based cell shows smaller current signals, indicating weaker side reactions at the interface. The underlying reasons should be attributed to the protective function of Li3OCl originating from its small electronic conductivity and wide electrochemical window.

Subsequently, Li|electrolyte|Li symmetric cells were measured by EIS for five times during a period of 72 h to temporally monitor their self-aging impedances at open circuit potential (Fig. 6(e-f)). The total resistance values of both cells monotonically increase with time, indicating the continuous changing of the Li|electrolyte interface. However, the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li shows much smaller absolute magnitude and growth rate, compared with the Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li cell. In detail, the latter starts from 53 Ω at 0 h and increases to 65/80/88/94 Ω at 6/24/48/72 h, showing a growth rate of 77% in total and 1.07% per hour. In contrast, the former starts from 41 Ω at 0 h and increases to 43/48/50/52 Ω at 6/24/48/72 h, showing a growth rate of 27% in total and 0.38% per hour. The relaxation time distribution (DRT) was used to further analyze EIS results. DRT does not depend on the design of equivalent circuits, emerging as an advanced and intuitive method for analyzing these “irregular” impedance data, as reported in literature [52,53]. According to DRT results (Fig. S15), eight different relaxation times can be distinguished, among which τ1 represents the grain boundary response of electrolytes. And τ2-τ8 represent the diverse interfacial transition of lithium, electrolytes, and medium products during electrochemical processes. For Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li, the intensity of peak τ1 increases drastically with aging time (4/41/21/52/62 Ω at 0/6/24/48/72 h), as shown by the inset of Fig. S15(a). For Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li, the intensity of peak τ1 increases slowly (13/11/11/19/20 Ω at 0/6/24/48/72 h), as shown by the inset of Fig. S15(b). These time-resolved EIS and DRT results suggest that the Li3OCl shell can protect the Li6PS5Cl core from being attacked by metallic lithium to maintain stable electrolyte/lithium interface, and in turn greatly suppressing the growth of lithium dendrites [54]. In addition, in situ EIS of Li|electrolytes|Li symmetric cells were tested after different cycle durations under the current density of 0.1 mA cm−2 to investigate the structural evolution of the interface, as shown in Fig. S16. Initially (0 h), two cells show comparable impedance values, i.e., 41 Ω for Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li and 42 Ω for Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li. After 50 h, due to the creep of lithium metal, the impedance values decreased to 37 Ω and to 34 Ω, respectively. In the subsequent cycling, the Li|Li6PS5Cl|Li cell became unstable, with the impedance increasing to 41 Ω at 100 h and 43 Ω at 150 h, respectively, due to some side reactions at the interface. In contrast, the Li|Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl|Li remained stable, with the impedance maintaining at 34 Ω as the cycle proceeded, which can be attributed to the protection effect of Li3OCl to prevent Li6PS5Cl from being attacked by Li [55].

Finally, theoretical computations were used to analyze the electronic structures of the Li6PS5Cl(100)/Li3OCl(100) interface. The Bader charge analysis shows that the S-atoms on the Li6PS5Cl surface receives approximately additional 0.02 e at the Li6PS5Cl/Li3OCl interface, compared with the surface S of Li6PS5Cl. This result indicates a certain degree of reduction of S, being consistent with the XPS observation in Fig. 6(a-b). The subsequent charge density difference (Δρ) analysis was performed, as shown in Fig. 7 . For the direct contact between Li6PS5Cl(100) and Li(100) shown in Fig. 7(a), by setting the zero plane at 0.5 Å above the Li6PS5Cl surface, the section from 0 to 0.48 Å represents the interfacial region between Li6PS5Cl (bottom) and Li (top). The combination of S on the surface of Li6PS5Cl(100) with Li on the surface of Li(100) results in a significant reduction of S, with a charge gain of approximately 1.9 e. Consequently, the charge gain and loss with non-zero Δρ at the interface of Li6PS5Cl(100)/Li(100) is approximately 16 Å (indicated between two lines in Fig. 7(b)), i.e., the region where electrons can penetrate to induce the growth of lithium dendrites [56], wherein the corresponding depth inside Li6PS5Cl is 6.5 Å. In contrast, the situation in the case of Li6PS5Cl(100)/Li3OCl becomes much improved (Fig. 7(c-d)). Also by setting the zero plane at 0.5 Å above the Li6PS5Cl surface, the interfacial region between Li6PS5Cl (bottom) and Li3OCl (top) corresponds to the area from −0.46 to 1.53 Å, as shown in Fig. 7(c). The charge gain and loss mainly occur at approximately in the range of −2.50 – 3.02 Å (5.52 Å as indicated between two lines in Fig. 7(d)), affecting the Li6PS5Cl electrolyte only within 2.0 Å, less than 1/3 of the magnitude at the Li6PS5Cl(100)/Li(100) interface. The influenced region on the Li3OCl side is within 2 Å, smaller than the value of 2.5 Å depth for the Li3OCl(100)/Li(100) interface as reported in literature [56]. It is known that if the charge gain/loss at the interface takes place in a larger region, a thicker electrolyte layer will be required to prevent electrons flow from the electrode to the electrolyte to eventually result in a short circuit in the battery [57]. Therefore, from the above results, we can draw a series of conclusions as below. Firstly, Li3OCl has an electronic-insulating capability, which is consistent with its low electronic conductivity in bulk phase as measured (Fig. S1). Secondly, the Li3OCl shell can greatly alleviate the penetration of electrons into the Li6PS5Cl core. Thirdly, the Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl interface can significantly suppress the growth of lithium dendrites, as observed by the enormously enhanced CCD, cyclability and rate capability. In short, the theoretical computations can explain the experimentally observed performance enhancements. Moreover, the interface formation energy (E
IFE ) between Li6PS5Cl and Li3OCl was calculated on the thermodynamic basis to understand the underlying reasons for hand-grinding induced Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure, because hand-grinding pressure is too weak (on the order of ∼1 MPa) to be the driving force. The E IFE is defined as follows:
E
I F E
=
(
E
L i
6
P
S 5
C l /
L i
3
O C l
−
E
L i
6
P
S 5
C l
−
E
L i
3
O C l
)
2 A
where
E
L i
6
P
S 5
Cl /
Li 3
OCl
,
E
L i
6
P
S 5
Cl
and
E
L
i 3
O C l
are the total energies of the Li6PS5Cl/Li3OCl, the Li6PS5Cl and the Li3OCl respectively, A represents the interfacial area. The structure of Li6PS5Cl/Li3OCl interface is schematically shown in Fig. S17. According to the calculation results, E
IFE is −0.686 J m−2, which means that the Li6PS5Cl/Li3OCl interface formation is an exothermic and thermodynamically-favorable process [58,59]. Because the interface formation involves moving only one atomic layer on each side, equivalent to 0.62 nm thickness, the generated pressure from the corresponding E
IFE can be calculated to be 1088 MPa. Such pressure is three times higher than the pressure needed for compressing electrolyte powder into pellets. Thus, all the studies together show that the underlying reasons for obtaining Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure are the thermodynamically favorable E IFE between them and good ductility and low hardness of Li3OCl.

In conclusion, this work has reported a simple yet effective interface-engineering strategy to greatly enhance the electrochemical performance of Li6PS5Cl, a widely-studied sulfide solid electrolyte. The strategy is to coat the Li6PS5Cl electrolyte surface with another electrolyte Li3OCl to prepare a Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl core@shell structure, which is realized by manually grinding the materials mixture for just a few minutes. Although the mass portion of Li3OCl is merely 5% and the shell is only ∼50 nm thick, the Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl interface generates a synergistic effect through the electron donation of Li3OCl to Li6PS5Cl. Consequently, the Li3OCl shell featured with low electronic conductivity as well as high stability against both Li anode and NCM721 cathode can not only circumvent the direct contact of Li metal with Li6PS5Cl but also suppress the dendrites growth inside Li6PS5Cl; meanwhile, the Li6PS5Cl core with high ionic conductivity can conduct Li-ions under higher current rates with longer cycling stability. For instance, the CCD value of Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl in Li-symmetric cells at 30 °C is 2.67 times as that of L6PS5Cl, reaching up to 1.6 mA cm−2, and also can cycle steadily for over 1400 h (900 h) at 0.2 mA cm−2 (0.5 mA cm−2), respectively. In Li|electrolyte|NCM721 full cells, Li6PS5Cl@Li3OCl enables to cycle up to 2.0 C at 30 °C (or 3.0 C at 60 °C), while Li6PS5Cl cannot cycle beyond 0.5 C and 1.0 C, respectively. The above observed experimental results are strongly supported by the theoretical calculations on the electronic structures of electrolytes. Because the reported strategy herein utilizes simple materials and easy process, it has the potential to be adapted for developing practically high-performance Li6PS5Cl to expedite the development of commercial ASSLBs.

Yuzhe Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Haolong Chang: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Aiguo Han: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shijie Xu: Investigation, Data curation. Xinyu Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Shunjin Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Xiaohu Hu: Investigation, Data curation. Yujiang Sun: Investigation, Data curation. Xiao Sun: Investigation, Data curation. Xing Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software. Yongan Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFE0111600). We thank the Haihe Laboratory of Sustainable Chemical Transformations for financial support. This work was also partially supported by the Graduate Top-notch Innovation Award Plan in Liberal Arts and Science of Tianjin University for the Year of 2023 (B2-2023-012). This work is dedicated to the 130th anniversary of Tianjin University and the 100th anniversary of its Discipline of Chemical Engineering and Technology.
